title: The Three Sentences
description: Each evening write exactly three sentences — no more — about your day.
steps: 1. Open your notebook at the end of the day|2. Write: What drained me today — one specific thing, not a general feeling|3. Write: What gave me even a small amount of energy today — however small|4. Write: What my body was doing that my words were not saying|5. Close the notebook. Do not analyse. Just notice.
goal: Building the habit of self-observation without self-judgement
success: Three sentences written, honest, specific — not general

title: The Energy Map
description: For one full week track what gives and takes energy — then map it.
steps: 1. Keep a small notebook with you for 7 days|2. Every time you notice an energy shift — up or down — write: what were you doing, who were you with, what time was it|3. At the end of the week lay it all out|4. Look for patterns — not explanations yet, just patterns|5. Write three observations about what you see
success: A completed week of notes and three written observations about the pattern

title: The Instinct Log
description: Every day for two weeks, note one moment where you had an instinct and what you did with it.
steps: 1. At the end of each day, think back — was there a moment where you sensed something before you understood it?|2. Write: what the instinct was, what you did with it — followed it, ignored it, overrode it|3. After two weeks, review — what happened when you followed it versus when you did not|4. Write one honest sentence about what you notice
goal: Rebuilding trust in your own perception
success: 14 days of instinct notes and one honest summary sentence

title: Name the Feeling Precisely
description: Once a day, stop and name exactly what you are feeling — not good or bad but the precise word.
steps: 1. Set a phone reminder for a time you are usually at work|2. When it goes off, stop for 30 seconds|3. Ask: what am I actually feeling right now?|4. Do not accept fine or stressed — find the precise word. Frustrated. Overlooked. Cautiously hopeful. Flat.|5. Write it down. That is the whole task.
success: One precise feeling word written down each day — not a generic one

title: One Real Thing
description: Say one genuinely real thing to one of the two people you already identified as safe.
steps: 1. Choose one of your two people|2. Observe them for a day or two first — what are they working on, what did they say in a meeting, what do they seem to care about|3. Find one genuinely curious or real thing to say — not small talk|4. Say it. One sentence is enough.|5. Write afterward: what happened, how it felt
goal: Rebuilding social presence through one small genuine moment
success: You said one real thing to one person and wrote what happened

title: The Fairness Inventory
description: Write an honest account of three times the system was genuinely unfair to you.
steps: 1. Find 30 minutes alone|2. Write three specific situations where you were treated unfairly — not your perception, situations where on reflection it was genuinely unjust|3. For each one write: what happened, what you did, what the outcome was, what you concluded about yourself as a result|4. Look at the conclusions — are they still running?|5. Write: which of these conclusions am I still living as if they are true?
goal: Separating genuine external injustice from internalised self-judgement
success: Three written accounts plus a list of conclusions you drew and which ones are still active

title: Map the Real Structure
description: In your current workplace, spend one week quietly mapping who actually holds influence.
steps: 1. For one week observe — not participate, just observe|2. Notice: whose suggestions get adopted in meetings|3. Notice: who people go to informally when something needs doing|4. Notice: who speaks last and is listened to most carefully|5. Draw a simple map — not the org chart, the real one|6. Keep it private
goal: Institutional literacy — reading the real operating system
success: A private written map of informal influence in your current workplace

title: The One Sentence Contribution
description: In one meeting this week, contribute one sentence. Not a speech. One sentence.
steps: 1. Choose a meeting where you currently say nothing|2. Prepare one genuine observation or question in advance|3. Find a natural moment — do not force it|4. Say the one sentence|5. Stop. Do not over-explain or follow up immediately.|6. Write afterward: what happened in the room after you spoke
goal: Visibility tolerance — being present without performing
success: You contributed one sentence in a meeting and noticed what happened

title: The Values List
description: Write your actual values — not aspirational ones, real ones evidenced by your behaviour.
steps: 1. Get 30 minutes alone|2. Write: what have I actually chosen when it cost me something?|3. Each answer is a real value — not what you wish you valued but what you demonstrably do|4. Write at least five|5. For each one write the specific situation that proved it|6. This list is your internal anchor — keep it
goal: Building a specific, evidence-based internal anchor
success: A list of at least five values each with a specific real situation that evidences it

title: The Stillness Practice
description: Practise physical stillness in low-stakes moments so it is available in high-stakes ones.
steps: 1. Choose one situation per day where you would normally fidget, look away or physically shrink — a difficult conversation, a moment of mild tension|2. Consciously choose stillness — feet flat, hands still, eye contact held gently|3. Notice what it feels like from the inside|4. Notice what changes in the other person|5. Write one observation
goal: Building physical presence as a genuine state not a performance
success: You practised conscious stillness in at least one moment per day and wrote one observation

title: The Small Bet
description: Once a week, act on one instinct in a low-stakes situation without overthinking it.
steps: 1. Choose something genuinely low stakes — a small decision, a minor opinion offered, a tiny action taken on instinct|2. Act on the instinct before you have talked yourself out of it|3. Note the outcome — not whether it was perfect but whether anything bad actually happened|4. You are building a new evidence base for your nervous system. Small bets. Small proofs.
goal: Rebuilding self-trust through accumulated small evidence
success: One instinct acted on per week with outcome noted

title: The Presence Walk
description: Walk into a room you find difficult and practise occupying your full space.
steps: 1. Choose a room or meeting where you usually make yourself small|2. Before entering, take three slow breaths|3. Enter at your normal pace — do not rush or hesitate|4. Choose where to sit deliberately rather than defaulting to the edge|5. Sit with feet flat, take up your actual space|6. Do not perform confidence — just occupy the space that is genuinely yours
success: You entered a difficult room deliberately, chose your position, and sat without shrinking

title: The Rule Audit
description: Look at the formal rules in your workplace and identify which are load-bearing and which are discretionary.
steps: 1. Write down five rules or norms in your workplace|2. For each one ask: what actually happens when someone breaks this?|3. Categorise each as load-bearing — real consequences — or discretionary — selectively enforced or widely ignored|4. Note which ones you have been following rigidly that others treat as optional|5. Decide consciously what your approach to each will be
goal: Institutional literacy — knowing which rules are real
success: A written audit of five workplace rules with honest categorisation and your conscious decision about each

title: The Non-Reaction
description: Practise giving a non-reaction to a minor provocation.
steps: 1. This week, when someone says something mildly dismissive or irritating, try this: pause one second longer than feels natural|2. Respond to the content not the tone — as if you did not notice the edge|3. Keep your voice at exactly the same pace and volume as before they spoke|4. Write afterward: what happened next in the conversation?|5. Notice: did they escalate or did the energy dissipate?
goal: Responding to dominance without either fighting or shrinking
success: You gave a deliberate non-reaction to one provocation and wrote what happened

title: The No-Fear Audit
description: Identify three situations you currently avoid because of fear and audit what the fear is actually protecting.
steps: 1. Write three situations you avoid — meetings, conversations, social moments, professional opportunities|2. For each one write: what do I think will happen?|3. Then write: what is the realistic probability of that actually happening?|4. Then write: and if it did happen, could I survive it?|5. The fear is often protecting you from something that would not actually destroy you. Name it precisely.
goal: Dismantling fear by examining it specifically rather than avoiding it generally
success: Three written fear audits with honest probability and survival assessments
